title: Embodied Robotics Social Square Salon
skill_description: Run an embodied robotics communication-pattern reduction across specialist MoonBooks and return concrete research directions home.
review_reason: keeper reviews novelty, feasibility, evidence gaps, owner books, and whether the idea is worth becoming a long-horizon research program
quality_rules: A good output is not a generic robotics report; it is a cross-area exchange result.
Every idea must connect at least two specialist MoonBooks and expose a dependency, tension, missing interface, benchmark gap, or review gate.
Every idea must include testable research questions and a smallest useful next experiment.
Separate domain insight from operational chatter and never count infrastructure logs as research progress.
Preserve plurality: specialist books may disagree before the building reduces their views into reviewable directions.
Do not treat reduced ideas as accepted facts; return them to owner books for follow-up review.
skill_rules: Read every participant perspective before reducing.
Look for cross-area bottlenecks that no single specialist book can solve alone.
Prefer ideas that can become benchmarks, schemas, architecture contracts, experiments, or safety gates.
Reject vague combinations like 'combine VLA and world models' unless the output names a concrete failure mode and test.
Return each idea to the most relevant home books with an explicit next artifact.

participants.title: Large Models Desk
participants.focus_area: robot foundation models and multimodal pretraining
participants.perspective: Foundation models can contribute reusable semantic and behavioral priors, but robotics needs falsifiable embodiment assumptions instead of confidence-only language priors.
participants.current_question: Which parts of robotic intelligence should be learned as general priors, and which must remain robot-specific?
participants.contribution: Large-model work contributes semantic priors, multimodal grounding, synthetic task variation, and failure explanations that other robotics areas can test.

participants.title: Agent Systems Desk
participants.focus_area: planner-agent loops, tool use, memory, and self-correction
participants.perspective: Robots need agent loops that can recover, ask for help, and remember useful failures without accumulating unsafe habits.
participants.current_question: What memory and review loop lets a robot agent improve without quietly accumulating unsafe habits?
participants.contribution: Agent-system work contributes planning loops, memory promotion rules, self-correction patterns, and operator-review interfaces.

participants.title: VLA Desk
participants.perspective: VLA systems are promising but brittle unless they are evaluated on long-horizon recovery under changed layouts, tools, and lighting.
participants.current_question: How can VLA policies be tested for long-horizon recovery under changed layouts, tools, and lighting?
participants.contribution: VLA work contributes instruction-following failure cases, perception-action grounding gaps, and policy interfaces for high-level requests.

participants.title: World Models Desk
participants.focus_area: predictive dynamics, spatial memory, and counterfactual simulation
participants.perspective: A useful robot world model should veto risky actions before contact, occlusion, or tool failure becomes physical damage.
participants.current_question: Can a robot reject an action plan because its world model predicts contact, occlusion, or tool failure?
participants.contribution: World-model work contributes predictive uncertainty, counterfactual rollouts, spatial memory, and pre-action failure forecasts.

participants.title: Locomotion Desk
participants.focus_area: mobility, balance, terrain adaptation, and whole-body control
participants.perspective: High-level agents must request movement through interfaces that respect balance, terrain, energy, and physical safety constraints.
participants.current_question: What interface lets high-level agents request movement without breaking balance, energy, or safety constraints?
participants.contribution: Locomotion work contributes feasibility constraints, terrain adaptation signals, balance failures, and whole-body safety limits.

participants.title: Manipulation Desk
participants.focus_area: grasping, dexterity, tool use, contact, and tactile feedback
participants.perspective: Useful robot intelligence depends on contact dynamics and recovery from grasp failures, not only language-level task plans.
participants.current_question: Which tactile and force signals should be promoted into language-visible robot memory?
participants.contribution: Manipulation work contributes tactile failures, force signatures, tool affordances, grasp uncertainty, and contact-rich recovery examples.

participants.title: Robot Brain Desk
participants.focus_area: runtime architecture, arbitration, memory, policy routing, and safety envelope
participants.perspective: Embodied systems need an explicit arbitration layer between policies, world-model warnings, controller constraints, memory, and safety gates.
participants.current_question: How should the robot brain arbitrate between VLA policy, world model warning, and safety gate?
participants.contribution: Robot-brain work contributes runtime contracts, routing policies, memory scope, review gates, and explanation records.

participants.title: Data Engine Desk
participants.focus_area: robot data flywheels, simulation, teleoperation, video, and failure mining
participants.perspective: The next robotics bottleneck may be data accounting quality: knowing which samples improve real transfer and which just inflate benchmarks.
participants.current_question: Which data should enter the robot learning loop after a failure, and how should provenance be tracked?
participants.contribution: Data-engine work contributes source provenance, failure-mining loops, teleoperation labels, synthetic data boundaries, and promotion gates.

participants.title: Sim2Real Desk
participants.focus_area: simulation transfer, domain randomization, digital twins, and real-world validation
participants.perspective: Simulation is useful only when it predicts which policies will fail safely or dangerously outside the simulator.
participants.current_question: What simulation signals predict whether a policy will fail safely or dangerously in the real world?
participants.contribution: Sim2Real work contributes transfer diagnostics, digital-twin boundaries, domain-randomization limits, and reality-gap measurements.

participants.title: Evaluation and Safety Desk
participants.focus_area: benchmarks, red-team tests, physical safety, interpretability, and operator review
participants.perspective: A robot is not mature until it knows when to stop, ask, recover, refuse, and explain why under realistic disturbances.
participants.current_question: What benchmark reveals whether a robot knows when to stop, ask, recover, or refuse?
participants.contribution: Evaluation and safety work contributes red-team cases, benchmark design, refusal criteria, interpretability needs, and operator review gates.
